metadata.title: Harmony in Chaos: A Comprehensive Exploration of Disruption and Sustainability Through NLP and Network Analysis

abstract: The widespread adoption of information and communication technologies, along with the digital shift, predominantly relies on the practical application of novel methods that shape the essence of innovation. While literature highlights the swift pace of digitalization, it also encourages us to consider the notion of "disruption" within the framework of sustainability. This paper aims to examine the disruptions in the innovation of processes associated with sustainable development. We present a rigorous (quantitative) analysis of relevant literature spanning from 1995 to 2023, focusing on three research directions: (1) establishing a recursive thematic structure to categorize periods in the literature; (2) identifying defining topics; and (3) determining lexical units that serve as connectors between thematic groups. Through the application of natural language processing and a social network approach, the results reveal consistent topics across years rather than time-specific patterns, with processes and impact evaluation emerging as central junction points.

paragraphs: The digitalization of systems and the massive use of information and communication technologies (ICT) have generated radical changes that affect the social, cultural, economic, and ecological spheres. The speed of digitalization brings with it several new challenges, including the potential to generate new value, but, at the same time, this makes difficult to measure effects. The challenge of digitalization is even more evident if you think of the crisis caused by the Covid-19 pandemic, which has triggered and accelerated new models of digitalization.

In this context characterized by increasing digitalization, innovation is the key mechanism for addressing future challenges (Chesbrough, 2003). In recent years, the focus of innovation for researchers, professionals, and the general public has shifted towards sustainable development systems and practices, related to environmental protection, resource efficiency, improving social living standards, and long-term economic competitiveness (Behnam et al., 2018). In particular, to address the issues of climate change and environmental degradation (Ripple et al., 2020), innovation takes the form of socio-technical changes that can improve several aspects such as water quality and quantity, soil fertility, reduce erosion, biodiversity loss, and frequency and impacts of natural disasters on health (diseases and deaths). In facilitating the transitional processes, in terms of climate change and new sustainable development goals (Santos & Coad, 2023) that inevitably impact economies, governments, and territories, the Transformative Innovation Policy (TIP) plays an important role. Indeed, TIPs represent a collection of proactive and strategic approaches aimed at fostering innovation and change, emphasizing awareness and knowledge, experimentation policies, and stakeholder engagement. A fruitful conversation on the connection between sustainability, transformation, and innovation started with the release of the Brundtland Report (WCED, 1987) in which levels of action and research were declined in various areas, such as environment and social transition (Jacobsson and Bergek, 2011); sustainability in systems (Geels, 2011); and business models (Boons et al., 2013). In this conceptualization, takes shape both the idea that sustainability is driving innovation processes (Melane-Lavado & Álvarez-Herranz, 2018), and that there is a deep connection between innovation and sustainability (Leal-Rodríguez et al., 2018). The literature on the sustainable transitions is mainly based on the promotion of rapid changes, through conceptual-analytical frameworks that are built around the dynamics of changes in socio-technical systems (Köhler et al., 2019). Thus, the technological innovation is described as a transition from the process of adaptation and conformity to a process of transformation (Kivimaa et al., 2021). The corpus of empirical and theoretical research that revolves around the transformative power of sustainable technologies, on the one hand, highlights the need to accelerate transitions and, on the other, stresses the importance of understanding the concept of "disruption" (Kivimaa et al., 2021) to critically assess its value, traps, and potential. However, the concept of "disruption" in literature has been used imprecisely, and the focus on its significance for sustainable transitions can be traced back to recent times.
The literature on innovation management emerged during the 1980s and 1990s for the first time.

According to Tushman and Anderson (1986) and Christensen (1997), disruptive innovation is a concept related to technological evolution that unfolds through periods of incremental change. During these periods, innovations can either enhance or destroy the competence of firms in an industry. Competence-enhancing discontinuities initiated by existing corporations may be associated with decreased environmental turbulence. Therefore, innovation is a crucial component of entrepreneurial action that transforms industries. Christensen and Rayner (2003), consider innovations as are characterized as small, steady advancements made by well-established companies that provide higher performance than in the past. However, other authors, such as Tait and Wield (2019), the concept of innovation may show disruption characteristics only for some sectors and not for others.

The main interest on the concept of disruption was recorded in 2018, when the focus shifts from the studies on disruptive innovation, to the "critical perspectives on destructive innovation and energy transformation" (Wilson and Tyfield, 2018). Kivimaa et al. (2021) shifted the focus towards environmental innovation and societal transitions by proposing a definition of disruption applied to sustainability. Their conceptualization is based on a high-intensity effect on the structure of sociotechnical systems. This effect manifests as a multifaceted, long-term shift that disrupts the functionality and stability of four main categories: (a) market and business models, incumbent technology and infrastructure; (b) regulations and policy; (c) actors, networks, and ownership structures; (d) practices, behaviors, and cultural models. The disruption of markets and business models reveals the close link between technologies and business profitability. New business models, in turn, represent an intrinsic example of disruption (Dijk et al., 2016). The business model can be configured as a result of disruptive technological innovation and disruption of the market regime. In the first case, the disruption reduces the value of existing resources and skills (Lindberg et al., 2018), generating a necessary change in the business model. The disruption and its impact depend heavily on how the public and business respond to change (Wilson, 2017). We can mention examples such as the development of autonomous vehicles on insurance industry business models and the disruption of electric mobility transition to energy and automotive markets (Berkeley, 2017;Skeet, 2018). Regulations, policies, and formal institutions configure themselves for the disruption through the removal of barriers caused by established structures for systemic change (Matschoss and Heiskanen, 2018); such as car sharing (Christensen and Rayner, 2003); the introduction of subsidies and infrastructure investments (Hoekstra, 2017); the regulations increasing environmental quality and energy use and efficiency (Ashford and Hall, 2018); and e-bikes that generate impacts upon users of other transport modes (Edge et al., 2020). The literature emphasizes a co-evolutionary dimension based on new actors entering a sector (Skeete, 2018). Examples are the changes in existing innovation alliances in city district development (Ashford and Hall, 2018) or active citizen participation in electricity production (Johnstone and Kivimaa, 2018). Transitions and innovations inevitably involve changes in cultural practices, behaviors, and meanings. However, these aspects are difficult to grasp and examine, so the literature is not sufficiently developed in this direction. Conceptually, Brown et al. (2013) identified the presence of leaders capable of disrupting existing behavioral patterns and generating new practices. The empirical cases in this area are primarily related to the urban area, such as the usage of electric cars (Hoekstra et al., 2017) or mobile apps in urban commuter behavior (Skeete, 2018).

In this landscape characterized by diverse forms of innovation and disruption linked to sustainability, with implications for businesses, business models, society, and cultural practices, this paper aims to analyze the primary domains affected by this paradigm shift over time. A bibliometric analysis of the scientific literature is proposed, involving the study and data integration of two bibliographic databases: Web of Science and Scopus.
The following three research directions are pursued through the bibliometric analysis:

(RD1) To identify a recursive semantic structure to classify the literature into thematic groups. (RD2) To uncover the main topics characterizing the thematic groups identified in RD1. (RD3) To explore the lexical features that play a central role or serve as bridges between the thematic groups.

To achieve these research objectives, techniques and properties from both Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Social Network Analysis (SNA) were integrated. This combined use of methodologies is one of the innovative features of this study for classification tasks and thematic group extraction. The multi-step combination of NLP and SNA proved advantageous for developing a more comprehensive semantic framework of the phenomenon. In fact, the two methods complement each other in framing the evolving semantic landscape. On one hand, document networks are used to identify temporal clusters of literature. On the other hand, word networks reveal the semantic relationships between texts. Specifically, this approach allowed us to: (a) identify document clusters based on the use of common words; (b) determine word clusters that compose the thematic groups; (c) uncover relationships between words and thematic groups, revealing the words that bridge the different themes.
Section 2 presents data description; Sect. 3 describes the model developed for data analysis; Sect. 4 shows the results; Sect. 5 presents the discussion and conclusion.

Forciniti et al. - 2025 - Harmony in Chaos A Comprehensive Exploration of Disruption and Sustainability Through NLP and Netwo
paragraphs: Our data collection is built on a data integration of literature from two databases: Web of Science (WoS) and Scopus (Sc). Combining these databases provides extensive coverage and ensures access to a wealth of prior research, minimizing the risk of missing relevant studies and strengthening the foundation of this work.

The data collection, conducted in October 2023, was carried out under the same conditions for both WoS and Scopus. All scientific products were extracted following the same keyword extraction steps. Two separate searches were conducted, one on each database, using the keywords "digital", "disruption", "impact", "sustainability". The selection of keywords was aimed at identifying a wide range of sustainability-related issues, including environmental protection, social equity, and economic development. While the noun "sustainability" and the adjective "sustainable" are often used interchangeably, in our case, the query was based solely on the noun "sustainability". This choice aligns with the research objective of identifying latent themes that encompass a broad spectrum of issues that have developed around practices and systems. In contrast, using the adjective "sustainable" would have narrowed the information source to specific uses and customs without problematizing the underlying issues. Similarly, the connotations associated with "disruption" and "disruptive" differ. While "disruptive" is primarily used to describe shifting paradigms or disruptive technologies that revolutionize an existing industry, our aim was to identify sudden and significant changes capable of upsetting the status quo. Therefore, "disruption" was the most effective keyword for extracting challenged existing paradigms or intercepting new theories and perspectives.
The query was oriented to data extraction of keywords within abstract and author keywords.

No filters of year of publication or access (e.g., open, enriched cited references, open publisher-invited reviews) was applied. Among document types, articles, review articles, proceedings papers, and book chapters were involved, and only products in English, without differences among the disciplines, were selected.

The records obtained from the WoS and Sc queries were integrated into a single database. This data search returned a large number of results, N = 3,145: 2,179 from WoS, and 966 from Sc. However, to perform a suitable data integration of two databases, the fields were aligned: title, author full names, year of publication, abstract, and index keywords were selected, while the remaining information was deleted. In addition, in data integration processes, it was highly likely that some records were duplicates. Through cross-checking of data, duplicates have been removed. When removing duplicates, the record was retained in the database that displayed the most complete information. Thus, the data collection assessed for eligibility is composed of 2,255 records: from Scopus 897 and from Web of Science 1,358.
The flow diagram in Fig. 1 shows the data extraction and data integration protocol.

sections.paper_id: Forciniti et al. - 2025 - Harmony in Chaos A Comprehensive Exploration of Disruption and Sustainability Through NLP and Netwo
paragraphs: The data collection consists of 2,255 scientific products spanning the period from 1995 to 2023. However, scientific contributions are not present for all years. Only 24 out of the 29 years were represented in the extracted data. Over these 29 years, scientific production on disruptive innovation shows a significant increase. The time plot (Fig. 2) highlights this trend. From 1995 to the early 2000s, there was limited interest in this topic. This can be partially explained by the fact that the concept of disruption in innovation emerged in the innovation management literature of the 1980s and 1990s (Kivimaa et al., 2021). Early research focused more on traditional technological innovation than on disruption. The earliest contributions, dating back to 1995, linked sustainable development practices with emerging computer technologies. This era saw the rise of the World Wide Web, characterized by data networks and hypertext, which facilitated a powerful and user-friendly global information system. The World Wide Web's potential as a platform for information sharing held significant promise for sustainability efforts, particularly in terms of its effectiveness as a communication tool. Indeed, the Web has accelerated the global exchange of information by creating national computer networks in many developing countries, including sustainable development networks. A connection to the development of software like the Global Change Data Center for environmental monitoring and modeling may be formulated. This relationship can be seen as an opportunity at offering to policymakers and scientists a better understanding of environmental challenges. Some authors explored these themes further. Coppin and Bauer (1995) highlighted the value of secondary information for resource management, while Christensen and Rosenbloom (1995) examined the impact on the entrepreneurial context, suggesting that companies slow to adapt might experience market share and revenue losses.

The period between 1995 and 2011 witnessed a growing focus on digitalization. This was partly driven by the launch of e-commerce giants, which revolutionized shopping habits, increased efficiency, and potentially reduced environmental impact. The 2000s also saw a growing interest in the environmental implications of technology. This trend culminated in the launch of the United Nations' Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) in 2011, which provided a global framework for addressing sustainability challenges and fostering global engagement. By 2012, the historical context was marked by the emergence of the sharing economy, smart grids for optimizing electricity distribution (Cicconi et al., 2012) and the adoption of cloud computing (Chowdhury, 2012). This period saw companies embracing the paradigm of data collection and analysis to study environmental impact, improve efficiency, and reduce waste, fundamentally altering the socio-economic fabric. Consequently, the literature from 2012 onwards emphasizes the growing use and potential of digital technologies, information and communication technology (ICT), green IT (Welfens and Lutz, 2012), e-government business models (Panagiotopoulos et al., 2012), enterprise interoperability (Jardim-Goncalves et al., 2012), and social media (Pan & Thomas, 2012) to address sustainability challenges. The year 2015 saw a surge in awareness of the environmental impact of consumption, leading to the rise of "green consumerism" trends. Additionally, this period witnessed innovations like the introduction of electric vehicles (EVs) in the transportation sector, the development of renewable energy sources (solar and wind power), and the emergence of circular economy models focused on waste reduction and product lifespans. From 2016 to 2019, the context was characterized by the development of new tools for the optimization of sustainability. Central topics were the creation of new markets and value chains on the manufacturing sector (Gamarra et al., 2016); the urban design and sprawl (Morollón et al., 2016); the reduction of environmental impact and the renewable materials (biogas, solar energy) (Neri et al., 2018); the additive manufacturing, smart cities (Webster and Leleux, 2019). The year 2020 was significantly shaped by the global health pandemic. Research focused on the potential of assistive technologies, such as digital health coaching (Santini et al., 2020), and crisis management tools (Ayman et al., 2020). The past two years (2022 and 2023) represent the largest portion of the data collection, centered on themes like the circular economy (Seegrün et al., 2023), greenhouse gas emissions (Ziegler & Hilborn, 2023), artificial intelligence systems (Samuel & Lucassen, 2022), and the bio-digital industry (Rotondi, 2023).

paragraphs: For the lemmatization, we used a pre-trained model built on Universal Dependencies treebanks that is made available for more than 65 languages and based on 101 treebanks (Straka et al., 2016). The procedure of text annotation or tagging for lemmatizing was based on all parts of speech (PoS). A criterion of exclusion was realized: the annotation was limited to nouns and noun compounds. This selection is directly linked to the following methodological step, which involves combining NLP with SNA for document classification. Document networks help us identify temporal clusters of literature based on the presence of similar words within their abstracts. In fact, previous studies (Bail, 2016;Rule et al., 2015) have shown that parts of speech as nouns and noun compounds are more useful in mapping the pivotal content of a text than other parts of speech, such as verbs or adjectives. To complete the pre-treatment, numbers, punctuations, English stop words (lists ISO 639-1 and SMART (Lewis et al., 2004)), and keywords used for data extraction were removed.

The feature extraction was based on the weight system TF-IDF (Term Frequency/ Inverse Document Frequency) (Salton and Buckley, 1988) whose strength lies in the discriminatory power of terms within the documents, and the lexical table called Document Feature Matrix was built. The lexical table A dxt is composed of q rows, where each row is a document represented by an abstract indicated as d (i = 1, …, q), and p columns, where each column indicates a term indicated as t (j = 1, …, p). The generic element d ji identifies the TF-IDF value of each j th term in each i th document:
where n ij denotes the frequency of j th term in each i th document; q is the total of documents, q j represents the number of documents in which the j th term is present.

The creation of lexical table A involved a crucial step: adding a new column for the year of publication for each document. This metadata allowed the building of a new "year x term" lexical table, in which, each row represents the publication year of an abstract, and each column contains terms. The rows corresponding to the same year were merged into a single row, essentially treating all documents published in that year as one document. This new lexical table, named D yxt , has years as documents in the rows, and terms in the columns.

Step 2 SNA The matrix D can be interpreted as an affiliation matrix, recalling the properties of SNA (Wasserman and Faust, 1994). SNA represents a method of analysis of social interactions widely used in Social Sciences to quantify the affiliations within a network of contacts in a given context (Wasserman and Faust, 1994). The methodological approach is also increasingly used to describe relationships between actors (Vilhena et al., 2014) that are carriers of a "cultural network" (Bail, 2016). In the direction of cultural trajectory, our D matrix is represented through a bipartite network that connects the documents, represented to years, to terms. The two structural elements that characterize the bipartite network are the nodes or vertices (as interchangeable terms) which identifies documents and terms, and their connections that are the ties. Formally the bipartite network takes shape through a bipartite graph G B (V 1 , V 2 , E), where V 1 and V 2 are the vertices belonging to two classes of entities, and E are the linkages (Borgatti, 2009). In our case, V 1 indicates the class of documents represented by years, V 2 denotes the class of terms, while E represents the ties between V 1 to V 2 .
Another approach to analyzing bipartite networks is to convert the bipartite data also called two-mode data into one-mode data projections. One-mode approach analyzes the

internal relationships in each class of entity (Borgatti, 2009). This is suggested when the analytical focus is on just one of the classes. In our study, in order to detect document clusters based on the use of common words, we transformed the affiliation matrix D into an adjacency matrix M, which contains both in row and column, the documents represented by year of publications. The matrix M represents a weighted squared matrix, which we obtained as D D T , where the diagonal was imposed equal to zero (Borgatti & Halgin, 2014). The visualization is represented by a graph G (V, E) consisting of a set of nodes or vertices (V) together with a set of links or edges (E) that connect them (Borgatti, 2009): V denotes the years, and E indicates the set of ties among them. Through the graph visualization, is possible to see the connections among the documents based on the overlapping of nouns and noun compounds. This network of linkages is able to capture the groups of cross-cultural intersections of innovation systems in sustainable development. Analogous approach is presented in literature. In fact, Bail (2016) used this perspective to study the messages of social media, creating a hypothetical cultural network in which nodes identified the actors engaged in conversation on an advocacy issue, while the ties between actors entailed the sharing of similarities in the content of their messages.

Furthermore, to better determine sub-groups of nodes that are densely interconnected with each other, and poorly connected with other parts of the network (Newman & Girvan, 2004) methods of community detection are often suggested in literature. This is the case with the aforementioned work proposed by Bail, which employs communitybased methods and algorithms. Community detection is the strategy presented by Bail (2016), where, starting from social media messages, he measured the "cultural trajectory" of each organization within the cultural network, defining links among advocacy organizations.

The literature offers numerous community detection algorithms (Newman & Girvan, 2004), each with its own characteristics, advantages, and disadvantages, depending on the specific case under examination. When choosing an algorithm, several criteria should be considered, including: (a) efficiency and scalability, as the algorithm's ability to quickly identify groups of nodes in large-scale networks involving large datasets; (b) adaptability, in terms of ability to handle different types of networks, including directed networks, weighted networks, and networks with overlapping communities; (c) multi-scale detection, to detect communities at different scales and granularities; (d) empirical performance: effectiveness in real-world applications demonstrated by empirical studies; (e) modularity optimization, ensuring a high-quality grouping of nodes; (f) programming language availability, making it accessible to a wider range of research.

In our study, aimed at detecting communities of documents represented by years of publications, a set of these factors were considered. Therefore, different algorithms were applied on M matrix, but one of the most significant criteria was modularity, which serves as a measure of the quality of a community structure.

It is well known that partition is achieved through the iterative maximization of modularity (Q) introduced by Newman and Girvan (2004) as a criterion to optimize the split in groups, leading to a hierarchical community structure. Specifically, Q modularity quantifies the extent to which a network can be partitioned into non-overlapping communities, computing as:

If we suppose to detect a thematic structure recursive that is able in classifying the times of literature, the adjacency matrix M may be divided into two communities: h is the total number of edges in the network; k i denotes the vertex i degree, represents the delta of membership value of the term i to the community c i . The value of modularity Q ranges in an interval [0; 1]. If the number of within-community edges is no better than random, we will get Q = 0; while values approaching Q = 1, which is the maximum, indicate strong community structure. Usually, empirical values of Q fall in the range from about 0.3-0.7 (Newman & Girvan, 2004).

With the aim of maximizing Q, several community detection algorithms were developed, as mentioned, whose optimization criterion has been a popular topic during the last decade (Clauset et al., 2004;Good et al., 2010). There is no exact optimal solution for every case, therefore, in our study, we applied several algorithms and selected the most effective to increase Q. The most efficient algorithms resulted the Louvain algorithm, the greedy approach introduced by Blondel et al. (2008). The execution of this approach is divided into two steps. In the first step, there is a local modularity optimization that takes place in each node's neighborhood. After this step, a new coarse-grained network is created by grouping the nodes in the same communities into super nodes. Iteratively, the processes are carried out in increasingly densely packed networks until node movement in the network results in an increase in modularity (Lancichinetti and Fortunato, 2011;Zhou et al., 2017). The Louvain method was chosen not only because it resulted in the best modularity for our data, but also due to its combination of efficiency, scalability, and effectiveness in identifying communities in large and complex networks. It offers fast processing times and has been empirically proven to perform well in real-world applications, including biological, social, and technological networks. Its multi-level aggregation allows for efficient detection of communities at different scales and granularities, and it is also adaptable to networks with overlapping communities.
For each community detected from Louvain algorithm, we have filtered the corresponding corpus, creating the sub-corpora of documents that are more similar for overlapping of nouns and compound nouns.
Step 3 Embedded Topic Model Sub-corpora: topic detection was performed on each sub-corpus derived from each document grouping of Step 2.

The aim of methods and techniques for topic detection is to discover hidden semantic structure in a collection of documents (Blei et al., 2003). Over time, the topic models and their further developments have been applied to many fields, from marketing to sociology, from political science to the digital humanities (Boyd-Graber et al., 2017). Most models are based on a generative statistical model belonging to Bayesian networks called Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) (Blei et al., 2003). LDA configures itself as a hierarchical probabilistic model, where each topic is represented by a distribution of terms and each document by a mixture of the topics. The topic proportions offer a low-dimensional representation of each document, whereas the topics themselves summarize the contents of a group of documents. Although it is the most widely used model in the literature, it is characterized by technical problems that often limit the study of large corpora. For this reason, topic modeling has become a subfield of NLP of its own (Dieng et al., 2020). LDA has been adapted for large textual collections through the adoption of variational inference and stochastic optimization (Hoffman et al., 2013).

Concurrently with strategies for detecting topics, the introduction of word embeddings was observed, aligning with the neural language model proposed by Bengio et al. (2003). In word embedding, the learning of a distributed representation means that the words with similar meanings are close in a lower-dimensional vector space (Bengio et al., 2006). In the last few years, word embedding methods have also been developed and scaled up to large datasets (Pennington et al., 2014) in different ways.

A strategy that enjoys the excellent properties of both methods is the Embedded Topic Model (ETM) (Dieng et al., 2020). This model proposed by Dieng et al. (2020) combines LDA and word embedding and represent the focus of the proposed methodology in this step, allowing at discovering an interpretable latent semantic structure of the documents and to provide a low-dimensional representation of the meaning of words through word embedding.

Like the LDA, the ETM is a generative probabilistic model, where each document is a mixture of topics and each word is assigned to a specific topic. However, compared to LDA, the per-topic conditional probability of a term has a log-linear form. This determines a low-dimensional representation of the vocabulary. Each term is represented by an embedding and each topic is a point in that embedding space. The topic representation as points in the embedding space identifies the robustness of ETM to stop words, compared to the most topic models. The ETM robustly accommodates large vocabularies. Dieng et al. (2020) compared the performance of the ETM to other approaches to demonstrate its better effectiveness. The comparison with LDA showed a better efficiency of ETM for large vocabulary. The ratio of the held-out perplexity on a document completion task and the topic coherence as a function of the vocabulary size has been performed both for the ETM and LDA. While the performance of LDA deteriorated for large vocabularies, the ETM had good performance, since the perplexity was normalized by the size of the vocabulary. Other comparisons were carried out with the neural variational document model (NVDM) (Miao et al., 2016), a form of multinomial matrix factorization, and PRODLDA (Srivastava & Sutton, 2017), a modern version of LDA that uses a product of experts to model the distribution over words, also with pre-fitted word embeddings. The findings of the experiments have led to better performance of ETM, both for predictions (Wallach et al., 2009) and for coherence and diversity topics. In ETM, Dieng et al. adopted an algorithm for approximating the posterior with variational inference (Hoffman et al., 2013) and used amortized inference to efficiently approximate the topic proportions (Kingma et al., 2014).
Step 4 Topic affiliation matrix:

Step 3 returned a series of thematic clusters for each sub-corpus, also called communities sub-corpora. Utilizing the Step 3 results, a new affiliation matrix was constructed. This new matrix is a topic affiliation matrix, indicated as B, where the rows are the communities c (i = 1, …, z) and the columns denote the terms constituting the topics a (j = 1, …, k). A binary relationship between community and topic was expressed through the generic element b ca :
The common terms among communities are inserted just one time. Thus, the topic affiliation matrix is a big matrix that unifies all topics for all communities.

Step 5 Topic affiliation graph analysis: at this step, a graphic visualization of topic affiliation matrix was proposed through a bipartite network, where V 1 are the nodes of class of communities, V 2 represent the nodes of class of topic, and E denote the ties among them.

Local measures of the topic affiliation network that are useful to detect which nodes are more 'central' than others, and to determine the structural features of a network. In this way, we can find which topics are more central, having an important role among communities.

b ca = 1 if there is affiliation between j th topic and i th community b ca = 0 if there is no affiliation between j th topic and i th community There are several measures able to explain the interactions among nodes, but among the most frequent (Faust, 1997), we applied the degree centrality that denotes the number of the connections of the nodes. The maximum possible degree for a community is the total number of topics, and the maximum possible degree for a topic is the total number of communities. In its version normalized, the degree centrality, (

where d j represents the degree of each community, n 2 the total number of topics for each i th community of the set of communities V 1 , while, d j indicates the degree of each topic, n 1 the total number of communities for each j th topic of the set of topics), V 2 . Another measure is the closeness centrality based on how close a node is to all the other nodes. The lowest score possible occurs if a node has a tie to every other node, in which case the sum of the distances to all others is equal to n-1. Its normalization ( c * i , c * j ) is equals:

A particular focus is to understand what topics and communities play a role in the interconnection of nodes in a network. We used the betweenness centrality that measures the number of times a node lies on the shortest path between other nodes. In this case, betweenness determines the paths from communities to communities, topics to topics, communities to topics and vice versa. The betweenness normalization ( b * i , b * j ) is equal to:

where b i is the betweenness value of each community in relation to the maximum betweenness of the set of nodes of the communities (b V1 ) for each i th community of the set of communities V 1 , and b j denotes the betweenness of each topic in relation to the maximum betweenness of the set of nodes of the topics (b V2 ) for each j th term of the set of topics V 2 .

Based on Kleinberg's authorities and hub conceptualizations (Kleinberg, 1999) in the World Wide Web, we determined authorities and hub centrality scores. The framework of this measure is to find broad search topics through the discovery of "authoritative" information sources on such topics. Hubs and authorities demonstrate a symbiotic relationship, wherein a strong hub refers to a web page that links to numerous reputable authorities, and a credible authority is a page that receives links from many well-regarded hubs (Kleinberg,
). We measured the importance of the topics with respect to the communities of documents. In this way, we can track the "scientific trajectory" over time.

The metric of cross-cliques connectivity (Faghani and Nguyen, 2013) has also been applied. This measure determines the connectivity of a node to different communities or cliques. If the value is high, its degree of interconnection will be high. The clique represents a maximal subset of the nodes such that every member of the set is connected by an edge to every other (Newman, 2010). The cliques can be overlapped if they share one or more nodes.

This metadata allowed the building of a new "year x term" lexical table, in which, each row represents the publication year of an abstract, and each column contains terms. The rows corresponding to the same year were merged into a single row, essentially treating all documents published in that year as one document. This new lexical table, named D yxt , has years as documents in the rows, and terms in the columns.

Step 2 SNA The matrix D can be interpreted as an affiliation matrix, recalling the properties of SNA (Wasserman and Faust, 1994). SNA represents a method of analysis of social interactions widely used in Social Sciences to quantify the affiliations within a network of contacts in a given context (Wasserman and Faust, 1994). The methodological approach is also increasingly used to describe relationships between actors (Vilhena et al., 2014) that are carriers of a "cultural network" (Bail, 2016). In the direction of cultural trajectory, our D matrix is represented through a bipartite network that connects the documents, represented to years, to terms. The two structural elements that characterize the bipartite network are the nodes or vertices (as interchangeable terms) which identifies documents and terms, and their connections that are the ties. Formally the bipartite network takes shape through a bipartite graph G B (V 1 , V 2 , E), where V 1 and V 2 are the vertices belonging to two classes of entities, and E are the linkages (Borgatti, 2009). In our case, V 1 indicates the class of documents represented by years, V 2 denotes the class of terms, while E represents the ties between V 1 to V 2 .

Another approach to analyzing bipartite networks is to convert the bipartite data also called two-mode data into one-mode data projections. One-mode approach analyzes the

internal relationships in each class of entity (Borgatti, 2009). This is suggested when the analytical focus is on just one of the classes. In our study, in order to detect document clusters based on the use of common words, we transformed the affiliation matrix D into an adjacency matrix M, which contains both in row and column, the documents represented by year of publications. The matrix M represents a weighted squared matrix, which we obtained as D D T , where the diagonal was imposed equal to zero (Borgatti & Halgin, 2014). The visualization is represented by a graph G (V, E) consisting of a set of nodes or vertices (V) together with a set of links or edges (E) that connect them (Borgatti, 2009): V denotes the years, and E indicates the set of ties among them. Through the graph visualization, is possible to see the connections among the documents based on the overlapping of nouns and noun compounds. This network of linkages is able to capture the groups of cross-cultural intersections of innovation systems in sustainable development. Analogous approach is presented in literature. In fact, Bail (2016) used this perspective to study the messages of social media, creating a hypothetical cultural network in which nodes identified the actors engaged in conversation on an advocacy issue, while the ties between actors entailed the sharing of similarities in the content of their messages.

Furthermore, to better determine sub-groups of nodes that are densely interconnected with each other, and poorly connected with other parts of the network (Newman & Girvan, 2004) methods of community detection are often suggested in literature. This is the case with the aforementioned work proposed by Bail, which employs communitybased methods and algorithms. Community detection is the strategy presented by Bail (2016), where, starting from social media messages, he measured the "cultural trajectory" of each organization within the cultural network, defining links among advocacy organizations.

The literature offers numerous community detection algorithms (Newman & Girvan, 2004), each with its own characteristics, advantages, and disadvantages, depending on the specific case under examination. When choosing an algorithm, several criteria should be considered, including: (a) efficiency and scalability, as the algorithm's ability to quickly identify groups of nodes in large-scale networks involving large datasets; (b) adaptability, in terms of ability to handle different types of networks, including directed networks, weighted networks, and networks with overlapping communities; (c) multi-scale detection, to detect communities at different scales and granularities; (d) empirical performance: effectiveness in real-world applications demonstrated by empirical studies; (e) modularity optimization, ensuring a high-quality grouping of nodes; (f) programming language availability, making it accessible to a wider range of research.

In our study, aimed at detecting communities of documents represented by years of publications, a set of these factors were considered. Therefore, different algorithms were applied on M matrix, but one of the most significant criteria was modularity, which serves as a measure of the quality of a community structure.

It is well known that partition is achieved through the iterative maximization of modularity (Q) introduced by Newman and Girvan (2004) as a criterion to optimize the split in groups, leading to a hierarchical community structure. Specifically, Q modularity quantifies the extent to which a network can be partitioned into non-overlapping communities, computing as:

If we suppose to detect a thematic structure recursive that is able in classifying the times of literature, the adjacency matrix M may be divided into two communities: h is the total number of edges in the network; k i denotes the vertex i degree, represents the delta of membership value of the term i to the community c i . The value of modularity Q ranges in an interval [0; 1]. If the number of within-community edges is no better than random, we will get Q = 0; while values approaching Q = 1, which is the maximum, indicate strong community structure. Usually, empirical values of Q fall in the range from about 0.3-0.7 (Newman & Girvan, 2004).

With the aim of maximizing Q, several community detection algorithms were developed, as mentioned, whose optimization criterion has been a popular topic during the last decade (Clauset et al., 2004;Good et al., 2010). There is no exact optimal solution for every case, therefore, in our study, we applied several algorithms and selected the most effective to increase Q. The most efficient algorithms resulted the Louvain algorithm, the greedy approach introduced by Blondel et al. (2008). The execution of this approach is divided into two steps. In the first step, there is a local modularity optimization that takes place in each node's neighborhood. After this step, a new coarse-grained network is created by grouping the nodes in the same communities into super nodes. Iteratively, the processes are carried out in increasingly densely packed networks until node movement in the network results in an increase in modularity (Lancichinetti and Fortunato, 2011;Zhou et al., 2017). The Louvain method was chosen not only because it resulted in the best modularity for our data, but also due to its combination of efficiency, scalability, and effectiveness in identifying communities in large and complex networks. It offers fast processing times and has been empirically proven to perform well in real-world applications, including biological, social, and technological networks. Its multi-level aggregation allows for efficient detection of communities at different scales and granularities, and it is also adaptable to networks with overlapping communities.

For each community detected from Louvain algorithm, we have filtered the corresponding corpus, creating the sub-corpora of documents that are more similar for overlapping of nouns and compound nouns.

Step 3 Embedded Topic Model Sub-corpora: topic detection was performed on each sub-corpus derived from each document grouping of Step 2.

The aim of methods and techniques for topic detection is to discover hidden semantic structure in a collection of documents (Blei et al., 2003). Over time, the topic models and their further developments have been applied to many fields, from marketing to sociology, from political science to the digital humanities (Boyd-Graber et al., 2017). Most models are based on a generative statistical model belonging to Bayesian networks called Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) (Blei et al., 2003). LDA configures itself as a hierarchical probabilistic model, where each topic is represented by a distribution of terms and each document by a mixture of the topics. The topic proportions offer a low-dimensional representation of each document, whereas the topics themselves summarize the contents of a group of documents. Although it is the most widely used model in the literature, it is characterized by technical problems that often limit the study of large corpora. For this reason, topic modeling has become a subfield of NLP of its own (Dieng et al., 2020). LDA has been adapted for large textual collections through the adoption of variational inference and stochastic optimization (Hoffman et al., 2013).

Concurrently with strategies for detecting topics, the introduction of word embeddings was observed, aligning with the neural language model proposed by Bengio et al. (2003). In word embedding, the learning of a distributed representation means that the words with similar meanings are close in a lower-dimensional vector space (Bengio et al., 2006). In the last few years, word embedding methods have also been developed and scaled up to large datasets (Pennington et al., 2014) in different ways.

A strategy that enjoys the excellent properties of both methods is the Embedded Topic Model (ETM) (Dieng et al., 2020). This model proposed by Dieng et al. (2020) combines LDA and word embedding and represent the focus of the proposed methodology in this step, allowing at discovering an interpretable latent semantic structure of the documents and to provide a low-dimensional representation of the meaning of words through word embedding.

Like the LDA, the ETM is a generative probabilistic model, where each document is a mixture of topics and each word is assigned to a specific topic. However, compared to LDA, the per-topic conditional probability of a term has a log-linear form. This determines a low-dimensional representation of the vocabulary. Each term is represented by an embedding and each topic is a point in that embedding space. The topic representation as points in the embedding space identifies the robustness of ETM to stop words, compared to the most topic models. The ETM robustly accommodates large vocabularies. Dieng et al. (2020) compared the performance of the ETM to other approaches to demonstrate its better effectiveness. The comparison with LDA showed a better efficiency of ETM for large vocabulary. The ratio of the held-out perplexity on a document completion task and the topic coherence as a function of the vocabulary size has been performed both for the ETM and LDA. While the performance of LDA deteriorated for large vocabularies, the ETM had good performance, since the perplexity was normalized by the size of the vocabulary. Other comparisons were carried out with the neural variational document model (NVDM) (Miao et al., 2016), a form of multinomial matrix factorization, and PRODLDA (Srivastava & Sutton, 2017), a modern version of LDA that uses a product of experts to model the distribution over words, also with pre-fitted word embeddings. The findings of the experiments have led to better performance of ETM, both for predictions (Wallach et al., 2009) and for coherence and diversity topics. In ETM, Dieng et al. adopted an algorithm for approximating the posterior with variational inference (Hoffman et al., 2013) and used amortized inference to efficiently approximate the topic proportions (Kingma et al., 2014).

Step 4 Topic affiliation matrix:

Step 3 returned a series of thematic clusters for each sub-corpus, also called communities sub-corpora. Utilizing the Step 3 results, a new affiliation matrix was constructed. This new matrix is a topic affiliation matrix, indicated as B, where the rows are the communities c (i = 1, …, z) and the columns denote the terms constituting the topics a (j = 1, …, k). A binary relationship between community and topic was expressed through the generic element b ca :

The common terms among communities are inserted just one time. Thus, the topic affiliation matrix is a big matrix that unifies all topics for all communities.

Step 5 Topic affiliation graph analysis: at this step, a graphic visualization of topic affiliation matrix was proposed through a bipartite network, where V 1 are the nodes of class of communities, V 2 represent the nodes of class of topic, and E denote the ties among them.

Local measures of the topic affiliation network that are useful to detect which nodes are more 'central' than others, and to determine the structural features of a network. In this way, we can find which topics are more central, having an important role among communities.

b ca = 1 if there is affiliation between j th topic and i th community b ca = 0 if there is no affiliation between j th topic and i th community There are several measures able to explain the interactions among nodes, but among the most frequent (Faust, 1997), we applied the degree centrality that denotes the number of the connections of the nodes. The maximum possible degree for a community is the total number of topics, and the maximum possible degree for a topic is the total number of communities. In its version normalized, the degree centrality, (

where d j represents the degree of each community, n 2 the total number of topics for each i th community of the set of communities V 1 , while, d j indicates the degree of each topic, n 1 the total number of communities for each j th topic of the set of topics), V 2 . Another measure is the closeness centrality based on how close a node is to all the other nodes. The lowest score possible occurs if a node has a tie to every other node, in which case the sum of the distances to all others is equal to n-1. Its normalization ( c * i , c * j ) is equals:

A particular focus is to understand what topics and communities play a role in the interconnection of nodes in a network. We used the betweenness centrality that measures the number of times a node lies on the shortest path between other nodes. In this case, betweenness determines the paths from communities to communities, topics to topics, communities to topics and vice versa. The betweenness normalization ( b * i , b * j ) is equal to:

where b i is the betweenness value of each community in relation to the maximum betweenness of the set of nodes of the communities (b V1 ) for each i th community of the set of communities V 1 , and b j denotes the betweenness of each topic in relation to the maximum betweenness of the set of nodes of the topics (b V2 ) for each j th term of the set of topics V 2 .

Based on Kleinberg's authorities and hub conceptualizations (Kleinberg, 1999) in the World Wide Web, we determined authorities and hub centrality scores. The framework of this measure is to find broad search topics through the discovery of "authoritative" information sources on such topics. Hubs and authorities demonstrate a symbiotic relationship, wherein a strong hub refers to a web page that links to numerous reputable authorities, and a credible authority is a page that receives links from many well-regarded hubs (Kleinberg,

). We measured the importance of the topics with respect to the communities of documents. In this way, we can track the "scientific trajectory" over time.

The metric of cross-cliques connectivity (Faghani and Nguyen, 2013) has also been applied. This measure determines the connectivity of a node to different communities or cliques. If the value is high, its degree of interconnection will be high. The clique represents a maximal subset of the nodes such that every member of the set is connected by an edge to every other (Newman, 2010). The cliques can be overlapped if they share one or more nodes.

sections.text:
The corpus consists of 2,255 abstracts, and is characterized by 71,508 tokens and 39,008 types. Some of the most common lexical statistics have been computed. The lexical diversity is measured in terms of Type-Token ratio (TTR), where the types are the number of unique words and the tokens are the total number of words used in the texts. The TTR results of 60%, denoting a good differentiation among the scientific products related to the disruption in innovation. paragraphs: The community detection of Fig. 4 is based on matrix M (refers to Sect. 3) that indicates the unimodal projection of class of node containing the documents that are represented by years. M is composed of 24 rows and 24 columns, and the corresponding graph shows 24 nodes and 276 ties.

The descriptive statistics on the network cohesion indicates a measure of density of 85% as possible ties; therefore, a strong cohesion. The distance concept is also examined through the average number of edges between any two nodes. The mean distance, which represents the average geodesic distance between all pairs of nodes, is very low and equals 0.009. The result is due to the high degree of cohesion.

The Louvain algorithm, identified in the methodological section as the most efficient algorithm for community detection, achieved an optimal modularity score of Q = 0.52. The partitioning process identified four communities of varying sizes. Details regarding these communities can be found in Fig. 4 and Table 1.

In Table 2, we can see the lemmas that determined the partitioning of documents. These terms represent a recursive semantic and lexical structure. The first group is focused on the digitalization of systems and measurement; the second cluster denotes interest on business and economy; the third community is based on impact assessment; and the fourth is oriented towards environmental and urban sustainability.

paragraphs: Applying the ETM to the entire corpus did not yield sufficiently specific topic embeddings. To reach our objective, we adopted the ETM on sub-corpora derived from the communities identified in methodological Step 2. This approach aimed to achieve embeddings with a stronger association to specific years and a more nuanced characterization of topics within each community.

The number of documents in sub-corpora varies in a range [234; 965]. 2000, 2013, 2015, 2018, 2019, 2020, 2022 Datum; Datum;practice;growth;resource;production;process;impact;ecosystem;renewable 4 2006renewable 4 , 2007renewable 4 , 2008renewable 4 , 2009renewable 4 , 2010renewable 4 , 2014renewable 4 , 2017renewable 4 , 2021 Water; Water;environment;construction;urban;design;area;soil, carbon, green Communities 2 and 3 are those characterized by a wider corpus, although they are not both the most numerous groups of nodes (see Table 1).

For each sub-corpus (derived from the communities identified in Step 2), we constructed a word embedding matrix, and represented the vocabulary in a lower-dimensional space. We focused on the top 50% of terms with the highest TF-IDF scores, and a hyperparameter for variational inference was set in dimensions 100 to facilitate topic learning. The ETM achieved good classification results, as evidenced by the loss function evaluation (Fig. 5). This indicates a well-performing model for all four sub-corpora, both on the training set (70%) and the test set (30%).

Five main topics was retrieved from sub-corpora of four communities, able in reconstructing the lexical trajectory of the scientific production from 1995 to 2023. The selection of the five thematic clusters relies on the optimization of semantic coherence in topic models, as proposed in Mimno et al. (2011). Higher coherence scores indicate that words within a topic frequently co-occur and are thematically related. Additionally, a crucial factor is how well each data point is assigned to its cluster. This helps distinguish overlapping clusters with higher uncertainty in semantic coherence from well-separated clusters.
The ETM on the four sub-corpora identified the top five topics and extracted the 300 most relevant lemmatized terms associated with a rank ≤ 15. Table 3 shows some terms with a rank ≤ 5.

The ETM to the sub-corpora proved to be a successful approach, as it yielded results with enhanced specificity and a deeper exploration of the domain under investigation. In contrast, community detection alone, while identifying four distinct and content-based Table 3 ETM for all communities and terms Community Topic 1 Topic 2 Topic 3 Topic 4 Topic 5 1 Manager; intervention; control; risk; service; … Investment; strategy; implementation; adoption; recycle; … Tax; dynamic; industrial; requirement; microfinance; … Challenge; reduce; green; manufacture; build; … Climate; change; ion; operation; discharge; … 2 Global; energy; future; technology; datum; … Resource; digital; implication; impact; business; … Covid; learn; teach; social; process; … Technology; soil; land; health; reduction; … Impact; assessment; problem; approach; innovation; … 3 Transponder; reinforce; marketer; burner; adaption; … Life; cycle; restrict; skill; forestry; … Process; optimization; minimize; efficiency; skill; … Solution; comparison; theoretical; trader; redesign; … Photovoltaics; aquaculture; distance; governmental; critical; … 4 Ecological; education; health; tree; regeneration; … Local; urban; rural; arrangement; intervention; … Benefit; regional; citizen; plan; development; … Economic; public; tool; investment; regeneration; … Movement; labour; people; european; forest; …

communities, resulted in a classification with a limited vocabulary. The four macro-areas identified were: (a) organization and policy management; (b) business and data measurement; (c) processes, strategies, and impact assessment; (d) environment, health, and territory. While community detection provided a specialized classification, the underlying vocabulary was limited of size. Therefore, the comprehensive picture is achieved by combining community detection as the first step and ETM as the second step.

In addition, we can affirm the better performance of ETM than most common methods as LDA (Fig. 6). The ratio of the held-out perplexity on a document completion task and the topic coherence shows the better performance of ETM than LDA (Dieng et al., 2020). The perplexity normalized according to the size of sub-corpora in the LDA grows as the vocabulary width increases, while it decreases in ETM.

sections.header_raw: Topic Affiliation Matrix and Graph Analysis
sections.header_norm: topic affiliation matrix and graph analysis
paragraphs: To analyze the affiliation between topics and communities, a bipartite network analysis was proposed. The results are based on matrix B, which reveals two distinct classes of nodes. One class represents the four identified communities, while the other class represents the 20 extracted topics, corresponding to the 300 terms. However, for a better graphic intelligibility, the bipartite graph of Fig. 7 shows the first 140 terms, and the four communities. 144 nodes and 165 ties were detected.

With regard to network statistics on cohesion, the density indicates 16% of all possible ties. A density equal to 0.16 denotes a low degree of cohesion. The standard deviation of 0.46 confirms high variability within ties, and the mean distance is equal to 3.26.
In the graph of Fig. 7, the community are represented as orange squares, the terms characterizing the topics, as grey circles, while the ties as conjunction lines.

A dense network of terms exclusive to each community is observed; while other terms belonging to multiple topics identify the points of contact among the different communities. These nodes are the so-called bridges, whose removal would mean the disconnection from the network.

For the first community, there are topics related to organizational management where the characterizing words are industry, tax, management, performance, microfinance, policy, investment, engineer, and storage. The second community is characterized by terms related to market, communication, tool, economy, resource, business, datum, reduction, and process that refer to the macro-theme of business and data management. The terms of the third community are referred to strengths, skills, customization, restrictions, minimization, and references to agriculture, photovoltaics, croplands, and landfills. This last community identifies a series of processes and strategies and, at the same time, a measurement of impacts. The fourth community recalls concepts of health, territory, and the environment. In fact, it shows a clear appeal to ecology, to forestry, to structural change, and to health. The thematic group deals with indicators, structures, regeneration, and evaluation processes and emphasizes aspects of European, state and local territory.
A comparison between Table 2 and Fig. 7 reveals some interesting insights. While community detection primarily identified themes, ETM uncovered underlying dimensions within those themes.

More properly, the first community highlighted methods, technologies, and stakeholders, whereas the thematic affiliations point towards management and measurement aspects. The second community: identified industry, business, and transformative models, aligning well with the second thematic affiliation that is focused on markets, economic resources, and business. The third community is focused on renewable resources, processes, and data analysis. This aligns with the high level of environmental and professional specificity observed in the third thematic affiliation that is based on sustainable practices (agriculture, photovoltaics, croplands, and landfills) and strategic skills. Finally, both the fourth community and the fourth thematic affiliation share a common thread related to environmental sustainability, with a focus on health and territory.
Graph visualization of Fig. 7 helps to explore the lexical features that play a central role or serve as bridges between the thematic groups of different communities.

In fact, we can see the first community that is connected to the second community through business, tax, innovation, design, problems, which identify the bridges between organization and management and between business and data management. Analogous, the first and third community are connected through institutions and benefits that are the conjunctions points with business management, strategic planning, and impact measurement. The tie between the first and fourth communities is based to words such as construction and benefits which realized the bridge for organization and management, territoriality and environment. The second community, characterized by data management and industry, connects to the strategic community (community three) through innovation, environment, costs, and education. Data management (community two) connects with the environment and the territory (community four) through the nodes of change, tools, approaches, activities, and development. Finally, the strategic processes (communities three) and the environment and territory (communities four) are related by approaches and benefits.

Centrality measures show that the community of business and data management (community two) is the one with fewer links than the other communities. It has the lowest normalized degree value, equal to 0.27 (29 ties). The community of processes, strategies, and measurement of impact shows a higher degree of centrality, with a normalized degree equal to 0.50 (48 ties). This means that the publications of the years belonging to community three (see Table 1) have versatile and common topics with other topics. Probably, this value also depends on the size of the community. In fact, it is the largest community (965 documents). Among topics, we can see that the nodes with the highest number of ties are related to the words benefit (with degree of 0.15), institution (degree of 0.08), approach (degrees of 0.07).
To enhance the readability of the paper, only the top twenty statistics on degree centrality, listed in decreasing order, are included in Table 4.
The closeness centrality confirms the centrality of community three, with the highest normalized closeness equals 0.43 (Table 4), and with regards to the words benefit.

With a focus on the role of intermediary, the normalized betweenness centrality (Table 4) suggests that the community three plays an important role between the different topics (betweenness equals 0.50). The most central topic is related to benefit (betweenness of 0.15) that represents the conjunction point among the different topics over time.

Kleinberg's hub and authority scores can reveal nodes that exhibit characteristics of both roles. This indicates that some nodes might be highly relevant across different topics because they connect to content that bridges multiple communities. However, communities Fig. 7 Topic affiliation graph: communities and topics terms themselves are not directly represented as nodes within the hub and authority rankings. Table 5 presents Kleinberg's hubs and authorities for the terms that constitute the identified topics. Notably, the term approach emerges as a central term, highlighting the diverse models applicable to various research contexts. Additionally, terms such as benefit, innovation, environment, increase, education, carbon, economic, business, challenge, industry, problem, and design are also prominent.

The cross-clique connectivity metric identified numerous cliques composed of communities and terms. As shown in Table 4, most cliques involve communities. However, isolating the community nodes reveals ten main cliques formed solely by terms (Table 6), with each clique containing at least two terms (dyads). Table 6 presents the cliques with the fewest nodes ( ≤ 20) , highlighting sub-graphs of topics related to: (a) transformation of the atmosphere into biomass and photosynthesis through business approaches and developments; (b) challenges and approaches involving institutions; (c) awareness, training, and strategy for the environment and health; (d) emissions, energy consumption, and the future, with a connection to the Covid-19 pandemic; (e) tools and processes for studying implications. Table 5 Kleinberg's authority and hub centrality of the first twenty nodes in decreasing order Node Kleinberg's authority and hub centrality Approach 1.00 Benefit 0.91 Innovation 0.82 Environment 0.82 To increase 0.82 Education 0.82 Carbon 0.80 Economic 0.80 Business 0.80 Challenge 0.80 Industry 0.80 Problem 0.80 To good 0.80 Design 0.80 Institution 0.73 Change 0.62 Model 0.62 Activity 0.62 Development 0.62 Provide 0.62 Implication, communication, medium, area, analysis, develop, process, datum, education, increase, environment, focus, innovation, tool, provide, development, approach groups. A review of scientific literature on innovation in sustainability, spanning the period 1995-2023, has allowed us to explore the outlined research areas. Through a multi-step model based on combination of NLP with SNA, we analyzed a database of 2,255 scientific products built by the integration of WoS and Sc. By combining textual analysis and community detection, the years of documents proposing a recursive semantic structure were classified.
Next, a machine learning approach known as ETM, which combines the characteristics of LDA and word embedding, was used to extract thematic clusters from the document groups.
Finally, a bipartite network analysis was proposed to investigate the affiliation between the ETM topics and document clusters.

The results revealed no evidence of a progressively evolving recursive thematic structure over time. Consequently, classifying the literature based on shared vocabulary fails to capture a sequential thematic progression. Instead, the grouping suggests the presence of recurring macro-themes that vary in their level of micro-specificity over time.

Four semantic structures emerge as labels for classifying the entire corpus of literature: (a) digitalization of systems, organizations, and policy management; (b) industry and data measurement; (c) processes, strategies, and impact assessment; (d) environment, health, and urban sustainability.

The analysis of the lexical units characterizing these macro-themes revealed keywords that act as bridges between the themes and document groups. Management and industries are linked to innovation and business practices, including policies and taxes. Innovationrelated benefits serve as bridges to organizational structures, measurement tools, and processes. Notably, strategic planning, environmental, and urban development connect with approaches and impact assessment. Thus, processes and impacts identify the pivotal semantic elements on which the literature on disruption in innovation from 1995 to 2023 is based.

The methodological approach, which combined NLP and SNA in multiple phases, proved successful in enriching the informational framework of the studied phenomenon. Indeed, an initial application of machine learning to the entire corpus demonstrated that the preliminary document categorization phase successfully revealed semantic specificities that were undetectable in the entire corpus. The results exhibited a very short vocabulary size and specialized terms within a broad conceptual mix. Community detection enabled the mapping of interesting macro-themes related to sustainability innovation and disruption. The ETM applied to sub-corpora yielded a duality of specificity and a deepening of the domain, detecting micro-themes. In conclusion, the methodological integration of the two approaches can be considered complementary to deepening the semantic framework when studying highly specialized documents peculiar to a specific context.

However, some limitations emerged within the proposed work. The generic selection of keywords for data extraction could be expanded in future research. In fact, further steps could involve selecting the emerged keywords from community detection clusters able to categorize documents, thereby refining the micro-level rather than macro-level framing of the studied topic.

Furthermore, a new research direction could involve integrating additional bibliographic databases beyond WoS and Sc. Finally, from a methodological standpoint, deep learning strategies could be implemented and compared to the machine learning approach proposed in this paper, in order to investigate new perspectives aimed at the thematic cluster extraction.

A comparison between Table 2 and Fig. 7 reveals some interesting insights. While community detection primarily identified themes, ETM uncovered underlying dimensions within those themes.

More properly, the first community highlighted methods, technologies, and stakeholders, whereas the thematic affiliations point towards management and measurement aspects. The second community: identified industry, business, and transformative models, aligning well with the second thematic affiliation that is focused on markets, economic resources, and business. The third community is focused on renewable resources, processes, and data analysis. This aligns with the high level of environmental and professional specificity observed in the third thematic affiliation that is based on sustainable practices (agriculture, photovoltaics, croplands, and landfills) and strategic skills. Finally, both the fourth community and the fourth thematic affiliation share a common thread related to environmental sustainability, with a focus on health and territory.

Graph visualization of Fig. 7 helps to explore the lexical features that play a central role or serve as bridges between the thematic groups of different communities.

In fact, we can see the first community that is connected to the second community through business, tax, innovation, design, problems, which identify the bridges between organization and management and between business and data management. Analogous, the first and third community are connected through institutions and benefits that are the conjunctions points with business management, strategic planning, and impact measurement. The tie between the first and fourth communities is based to words such as construction and benefits which realized the bridge for organization and management, territoriality and environment. The second community, characterized by data management and industry, connects to the strategic community (community three) through innovation, environment, costs, and education. Data management (community two) connects with the environment and the territory (community four) through the nodes of change, tools, approaches, activities, and development. Finally, the strategic processes (communities three) and the environment and territory (communities four) are related by approaches and benefits.

Centrality measures show that the community of business and data management (community two) is the one with fewer links than the other communities. It has the lowest normalized degree value, equal to 0.27 (29 ties). The community of processes, strategies, and measurement of impact shows a higher degree of centrality, with a normalized degree equal to 0.50 (48 ties). This means that the publications of the years belonging to community three (see Table 1) have versatile and common topics with other topics. Probably, this value also depends on the size of the community. In fact, it is the largest community (965 documents). Among topics, we can see that the nodes with the highest number of ties are related to the words benefit (with degree of 0.15), institution (degree of 0.08), approach (degrees of 0.07).

To enhance the readability of the paper, only the top twenty statistics on degree centrality, listed in decreasing order, are included in Table 4.

The closeness centrality confirms the centrality of community three, with the highest normalized closeness equals 0.43 (Table 4), and with regards to the words benefit.

With a focus on the role of intermediary, the normalized betweenness centrality (Table 4) suggests that the community three plays an important role between the different topics (betweenness equals 0.50). The most central topic is related to benefit (betweenness of 0.15) that represents the conjunction point among the different topics over time.

A review of scientific literature on innovation in sustainability, spanning the period 1995-2023, has allowed us to explore the outlined research areas. Through a multi-step model based on combination of NLP with SNA, we analyzed a database of 2,255 scientific products built by the integration of WoS and Sc. By combining textual analysis and community detection, the years of documents proposing a recursive semantic structure were classified.

Next, a machine learning approach known as ETM, which combines the characteristics of LDA and word embedding, was used to extract thematic clusters from the document groups.

Finally, a bipartite network analysis was proposed to investigate the affiliation between the ETM topics and document clusters.

The results revealed no evidence of a progressively evolving recursive thematic structure over time. Consequently, classifying the literature based on shared vocabulary fails to capture a sequential thematic progression. Instead, the grouping suggests the presence of recurring macro-themes that vary in their level of micro-specificity over time.

Four semantic structures emerge as labels for classifying the entire corpus of literature: (a) digitalization of systems, organizations, and policy management; (b) industry and data measurement; (c) processes, strategies, and impact assessment; (d) environment, health, and urban sustainability.

The analysis of the lexical units characterizing these macro-themes revealed keywords that act as bridges between the themes and document groups. Management and industries are linked to innovation and business practices, including policies and taxes. Innovationrelated benefits serve as bridges to organizational structures, measurement tools, and processes. Notably, strategic planning, environmental, and urban development connect with approaches and impact assessment. Thus, processes and impacts identify the pivotal semantic elements on which the literature on disruption in innovation from 1995 to 2023 is based.

The methodological approach, which combined NLP and SNA in multiple phases, proved successful in enriching the informational framework of the studied phenomenon. Indeed, an initial application of machine learning to the entire corpus demonstrated that the preliminary document categorization phase successfully revealed semantic specificities that were undetectable in the entire corpus. The results exhibited a very short vocabulary size and specialized terms within a broad conceptual mix. Community detection enabled the mapping of interesting macro-themes related to sustainability innovation and disruption. The ETM applied to sub-corpora yielded a duality of specificity and a deepening of the domain, detecting micro-themes. In conclusion, the methodological integration of the two approaches can be considered complementary to deepening the semantic framework when studying highly specialized documents peculiar to a specific context.

However, some limitations emerged within the proposed work. The generic selection of keywords for data extraction could be expanded in future research. In fact, further steps could involve selecting the emerged keywords from community detection clusters able to categorize documents, thereby refining the micro-level rather than macro-level framing of the studied topic.

Furthermore, a new research direction could involve integrating additional bibliographic databases beyond WoS and Sc. Finally, from a methodological standpoint, deep learning strategies could be implemented and compared to the machine learning approach proposed in this paper, in order to investigate new perspectives aimed at the thematic cluster extraction.
